title: Feedback Loops for Continuous Update
concept: Use logged user corrections to iteratively refine prompt templates, RAG indexing, or fine-tuning datasets over time.

exercises.prompt: What does a "feedback loop" in AI development refer to?

options.text: Using user edits to improve the AI over time.
Restarting the AI server every hour.
Writing longer prompts for the user to read.

exercises.explain: Feedback loops allow AI systems to ingest human corrections to improve future responses automatically.

cards.title: How AI Learns From You
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how AI assistants get smarter over time? They don't do it by magic! They learn from their own mistakes by listening to the edits and corrections that humans make to their work. Let's explore how to build this self-improving cycle.

cards.title: What is a Feedback Loop?
cards.body: A feedback loop is a cycle where you capture a user's corrections (like when they fix an AI-generated draft), store those corrections, and use them to update your prompts, search indexes, or training data. This ensures the AI gets better over time instead of repeating the same mistakes.

cards.title: The Recipe Diary
cards.body: Imagine owning a restaurant. If customers tell you a soup is too salty, you don't throw away the kitchen. You write down the feedback and adjust the recipe for the next day. Similarly, keeping user corrections acts like a recipe diary that makes your AI helper smarter with every single meal it serves.

cards.title: Real-World Loop in Action
cards.body: An AI customer support bot drafts an email response, but a human agent edits the refund policy section before sending it. The system automatically saves both the original draft and the agent's corrected version. At the end of the week, these pairs are used to update the bot's prompt guidelines.

cards.title: Myth: AI is One-and-Done
cards.body: Many think that once an AI is deployed, it stays smart forever on its own. In reality, AI models quickly encounter new situations or make repeated errors. Without a continuous feedback loop to ingest corrections, the AI's performance will stall or get outdated.

cards.title: Static vs. Adaptive Systems
cards.body: Compare a static system that never updates with an adaptive system that grows smarter using a feedback loop.

left.text: Ignores user edits. The AI repeats the exact same errors tomorrow.

right.text: Saves edits to update templates and training data. The AI gets smarter daily.

exercises.prompt: Why do we collect and log user corrections in an AI application?

options.text: To make the AI run faster on mobile devices.
To continuously refine the AI's prompts, data indexes, and training datasets.
To block users who make too many edits from using the system.
To completely rewrite the AI model from scratch every single day.

exercises.explain: Using logged corrections to update prompt templates, RAG indexes, or training data allows the AI to learn from its past mistakes and adapt to user needs.

exercises.prompt: True or False: User feedback loops can be used to improve prompt templates as well as RAG indexing systems.
exercises.explain: Feedback loops can be used to update RAG search databases, prompt templates, and even datasets used to fine-tune the core model.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how AI systems self-improve.
exercises.sentence: To build a continuous feedback loop, we must capture and save user corrections to update the system.
exercises.explain: Continuous improvement relies on capturing user corrections to update prompt templates or training data.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios demonstrates a true feedback loop for continuous update?

options.text: An AI assistant logs when a user edits its code outputs, and engineers use those edits to update the prompt templates weekly.
An AI assistant is trained once on Wikipedia and never updated again.
An AI assistant provides a 'Dislike' button that deletes the conversation immediately without saving any logs.

exercises.explain: Option C is incorrect because feedback should be structure-logged, not ignored. Option B shows a system that only learns once. Option A represents a continuous feedback loop where corrections are actively used to update the AI's core data source.

exercises.prompt: You are building a production AI system that needs to get smarter every day based on real-world usage. What is the best strategy to implement a continuous feedback loop?

options.text: Delete all logs weekly to save database space and keep the AI clean.
Hardcode every user correction directly into the main application source code.
Automatically save user edits to refine training data and prompt templates over time.
Prompt the user to rewrite the entire system prompt every time they find an error.

exercises.explain: Setting up a continuous feedback loop means user corrections are saved and actively used to improve the system's prompt templates, data index, or training datasets. Ignoring feedback keeps the AI frozen in time.
